close_reason: Split error into field-not-found vs field-not-a-string with json_type_name helper
description:
lib.rs:230-237 in get(): when ?field= is set, it does data_map.get(field).and_then(|v| v.as_str()). If the field exists but holds a number, bool, array, or object, as_str() returns None and the error becomes 'field <name> not found or not a string'. This conflates 'absent' with 'present but wrong type'.

Bad behavior: A caller who wrote {"port": 5432} via the JSON-object put path and then reads with ?field=port gets 'not found or not a string'. The user has to read the source to discover that ?field= only works for string fields.

Fix:
1. Distinguish missing vs. wrong-type in the error message: 'field <name> not found' vs. 'field <name> is not a string (got <type>)'.
2. OR support extracting non-string scalars by JSON-encoding them (so ?field=port returns b'5432').
3. Document the constraint explicitly in the module-level docs and README.

Pick (1) at minimum; (2) is a bigger API decision.
title: ?field= get rejects non-string fields with confusing error
